report:
Here is the assessment for this client.

---

## Your Health Assessment

What you've shared paints a picture that's actually quite coherent once you see the threads connecting it. You've described your goal as "cleaner energy day to day," and that's exactly the right frame. What follows is our read on what might be getting in the way.

---

### A Daily Eating Pattern That May Be Working Against You

One of the most consistent findings across your answers is a pattern we see often: skipping food through most of the day, then eating later. Physiologically, the body reads extended periods without food as a signal of scarcity and responds by [raising cortisol](https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2895000/) to generate glucose from its own tissues. Research also shows that fasting and caloric restriction can [significantly reduce T3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8419605/), the active form of thyroid hormone, as the body tries to conserve energy.

This matters because several of your symptoms, including cold extremities, brain fog, unrested sleep, and low drive, map onto exactly what a sluggish metabolic state looks like. Going most of the day without eating, then eating later, can also leave your liver with insufficient glycogen reserves to sustain blood sugar overnight. This is likely part of why you're waking to eat or drink in the middle of the night. Your body is reaching for fuel it doesn't have stored.

```
Extended fast through the day
        ↓
Cortisol/adrenaline rise to maintain blood glucose
        ↓
T3 suppressed → slowed metabolism
        ↓
Glycogen stores depleted by nighttime
        ↓
Blood sugar drops during sleep → stress hormones spike → you wake up
```

This single pattern can quietly amplify almost everything else on your list.

---

### Sleep That Isn't Restoring You

Your sleep picture is one of the most telling parts of this assessment. You're waking at multiple points throughout the night, both early and late. You wake hot and sweaty. You have trouble falling asleep. You wake unrestored. And you never dream.

Each of these points to something distinct, but they overlap. Waking in the first part of the night often reflects elevated stress hormones, particularly adrenaline, which spike when blood sugar drops too low. Waking later in the night points more toward gut irritation activating the nervous system. Feeling hot and sweaty on waking is a classic sign of hormonal fluctuation, particularly estrogen, during sleep. And never dreaming is significant: dreaming occurs during deep, restorative sleep, and its absence suggests the body isn't reaching the stages of sleep where repair and memory consolidation happen.

Research on [ovarian hormones and perimenopausal sleep](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S108707922200123X) shows that fluctuating estrogen affects thermoregulation through the hypothalamus, with nocturnal hot flashes coinciding with the majority of awakenings in women experiencing hormonal imbalance. You don't need to be perimenopausal for estrogen fluctuations to produce this pattern.

```
Estrogen fluctuation during sleep → thermoregulatory disruption → waking hot/sweaty (early night)
Gut dysbiosis / nervous system irritation → waking for no clear reason (later night)
Low overnight blood sugar → waking to eat/drink
        ↓
All of these fragment sleep architecture
        ↓
No deep sleep → never dreams → wake unrested
```

Poor sleep of this kind becomes its own driver of everything else, especially stress hormone levels the next day, gut function, and mood.

---

### Gut Dysfunction Starting at the Top

Your gut picture is worth looking at carefully. You have diarrhea most days, loose and poorly formed stools, and symptoms that worsen noticeably after most meals, immediately after eating. That timing is an important clue. When symptoms begin right at the meal, rather than one to three hours later, the issue often originates in the stomach itself rather than deeper in the small intestine. This points toward poor gastric function: possibly low stomach acid, poor gastric motility, or both, which then sets the stage for incomplete digestion and downstream fermentation.

The dental problems you mentioned are also not unrelated. The microbial balance in the mouth tends to reflect that of the gut, and significant ongoing dental issues like bad breath, gum inflammation, or cavities often accompany [gut dysbiosis](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8454481/).

Diarrhea that's chronic is rarely just about the gut in isolation. It connects to stress hormone levels (adrenaline and cortisol accelerate gut transit), microbial imbalance, and elevated gut serotonin, all of which are likely in play here.

---

### Hormonal Imbalance Showing Up Across Your Body

The combination you've described is a consistent hormonal signal: intense menstrual cramps affecting daily life, PMS, very low libido, night sweats, irritability, and significant skin issues. These often cluster together when estrogen and progesterone are out of balance.

The menstrual pain connection is well-established. [Prostaglandin overproduction driven by low progesterone relative to estrogen](https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5585876/) is a primary driver of dysmenorrhea, the type of cramping that disrupts daily function. Excess estrogen also promotes mast cell activation, histamine release, and systemic inflammation, which can affect skin, mood, and gut sensitivity simultaneously.

This is also where everything starts to connect more visibly. Gut dysbiosis itself can elevate estrogen by impairing its clearance. Chronic stress from undereating raises cortisol, which disrupts progesterone. Poor sleep keeps stress hormones elevated. Each system feeds back into the others.

```
Poor gut health → impaired estrogen clearance → estrogen excess
Chronic stress (undereating, poor sleep) → suppressed progesterone
        ↓
Estrogen : Progesterone imbalance
        ↓
Intense cramps / PMS / low libido / night sweats / skin issues / irritability
```

---

### Loss of Pleasure and Mental State

The complete absence of pleasure from activities you used to enjoy, combined with very low libido and your history of mental health struggles, is the part of your picture we'd be most attentive to. This isn't simply "stress" or "burnout."

Research on inflammation and brain function has shown that [elevated inflammatory cytokines directly reduce dopamine availability in reward circuits](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34971449/), producing exactly the flat, disengaged, joyless state you're describing. And the sources of that inflammation are likely already present: gut dysbiosis, poor sleep, chronic hormonal stress, and a diet pattern that keeps the stress system activated through the day. This is a meaningful mechanistic pathway, not just a mood issue.

The pounding heart and breath catching you sometimes notice are also consistent with a nervous system running hotter than it should, with stress hormones filling in for what stable energy supply and sleep should be providing.

---

### Putting It Together

Across all of this, the through-line is an energy system under sustained pressure: from the way the day is structured around eating, from a gut that's working against rather than with you, from hormonal signals that are amplifying inflammation, and from sleep that isn't providing recovery. None of these are isolated problems. They are the same problem expressing itself through different systems.

Your two supplements, magnesium and vitamin D, are genuinely relevant here. Both are anti-stress nutrients, and their absence would make most of this worse. But they're operating against a backdrop that has more layers than those two nutrients alone can reach.

This assessment draws from a brief snapshot, and some of what's described here could have multiple explanations. There's more that a fuller picture, and ideally some targeted testing, would help clarify. But the patterns here are real and they are connected, and that's a meaningful place to start.
